payload.summary: Batch A complete — 8 voter-facing posts reviewed. Part 1 only (factual/AR/defensibility). 2 posts require revision, 6 pass with notes. 5 systemic issues span the entire series. Part 2 (ethical marketing rubric) deferred to next turn.

methodology.ar_rubric: docs/adversarial-register-rubric.md — 3-dimension weighted scoring (dialogue mode 0.40, stance markers 0.35, attribution pattern 0.25)
methodology.defensibility: site-defensibility-review precedent — findings tagged F-N with severity HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
methodology.factual_verification: Each claim checked against treaty text, constitutional provisions, UN Treaty Collection, congressional record, and published research. Web lookups performed where available. Negative claims flagged for scope limitations.
methodology.scoring_context: 8 parallel review agents, each reviewing one post in isolation (no cross-post contamination). All agents applied identical rubric and output schema.

defensibility_findings.finding: One-sided framing — zero counterarguments for non-ratification presented
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add section acknowledging sovereignty, federalism, aspirational-vs-justiciable debate

defensibility_findings.finding: Enforceability overstated — 'treaty obligations with monitoring mechanisms' omits progressive realization (Art 2(1)) and non-binding nature of CESCR concluding observations
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Clarify that CESCR monitoring produces recommendations, not enforceable rulings

defensibility_findings.finding: AI displacement claim unsourced — 'at a scale that existing U.S. law was not designed to address' functions as urgency premise without citation
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite workforce impact studies or frame as projected risk

defensibility_findings.finding: U.S. RUDs practice omitted from ratification discussion — Senate routinely attaches reservations, understandings, and declarations
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Note that U.S. ratification would likely include RUDs limiting domestic legal effect

defensibility_findings.finding: '173 countries' count unsourced — UN Treaty Collection shows 171-173 depending on date
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite UN Treaty Collection with access date

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Fair-witness' self-description conflicts with advocacy structure (problem-urgency-action)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Revise researcher lens or restructure to include counterarguments

defensibility_findings.finding: Technical imprecision — 'Presidential signature on ratification instrument' conflates signing with deposit of instrument
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Replace with 'deposit of instrument of ratification with UN Secretary-General'

source_issues: Zero inline citations anywhere
No links to ICESCR treaty text, UN Treaty Collection, or Senate records
Only external link serves the call to action, not factual verification
batch_a_results.summary: Factually grounded advocacy that self-describes as fair-witness but omits counterarguments, overstates treaty enforceability, and cites zero sources for verifiable claims

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Majority of first-contact customer interactions' automated — quantitative claim unsourced, varies dramatically by sector
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite source or soften to 'a growing share'

defensibility_findings.finding: No counterarguments to ratification presented
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add brief section acknowledging opposing position

defensibility_findings.finding: Treaty enforcement limitations understated — no binding penalties mentioned for non-compliance
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add one sentence clarifying monitoring produces public accountability, not legal enforcement

defensibility_findings.finding: AI wage suppression 'decisively' overstates current evidence
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Soften to 'significantly' or cite labor economics research

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Second wave' temporal framing will age
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Flag for review if post remains active beyond 6 months

defensibility_findings.finding: Worker displacement categories lack citation despite drawing on published research (Brookings, WEF, Anthropic)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add parenthetical citation to at least one major source

source_issues: Zero inline citations
Self-referential links only (unratified.org)
Treaty articles paraphrased without linking to treaty text
batch_a_results.summary: Factually sound advocacy with structurally charitable framing; principal weakness lies in zero external citations and one unsourced quantitative claim

defensibility_findings.finding: No sources cited anywhere — systemic across ~10 factual claims
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add inline links for UN Treaty Collection, ICESCR text, congressional operations reference

defensibility_findings.finding: Missing RUD/self-executing context — sovereignty-concerned senators will raise this
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add brief explanation that U.S. practice includes RUDs limiting domestic legal effect

defensibility_findings.finding: Unnamed 'organizations that track ICESCR advocacy' — appeals to unnamed authority
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Name the organizations or reframe as general advocacy guidance

defensibility_findings.finding: '173 countries' staleness risk — count may have changed
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add 'as of [date]' qualifier

defensibility_findings.finding: AI displacement framing in letter template unsourced
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Soften to 'reshapes work' or cite source

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Never had coordinated advocacy at scale' potentially dismisses prior campaigns
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Qualify to describe contact volume rather than advocacy existence

defensibility_findings.finding: Channel weight hierarchy (in-person > phone > letter > form > email) unsourced
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite Congressional Management Foundation reports

source_issues: Systemic: zero external sources for ~10 factual claims
Only external link: senate.gov contact page
batch_a_results.summary: Well-structured advocacy guide with charitable attribution; weakened by complete absence of source citations and missing RUD context

defensibility_findings.finding: Factual error — CESCR reporting cycle stated as 'every five years' but shifted to 8-year cycle in 2022
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Update to 'periodically (currently on an eight-year cycle)' or note the shift

defensibility_findings.finding: Medical-debt bankruptcy 'exists nowhere else among ICESCR ratifiers' — universal negative across 172 countries without evidence
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Soften to 'a situation largely unique to the U.S. among wealthy nations' or provide comparative evidence

defensibility_findings.finding: No counterarguments to ratification presented — significant framing gap for a voter guide
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add brief section acknowledging common objections

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Highest per-student tuition costs among wealthy nations' — England surpasses U.S. for undergraduate public university tuition
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Qualify as 'among the highest' or specify graduate-level

defensibility_findings.finding: Student loan debt '$1.7 trillion' understates current figure (~$1.83-1.84T as of Q4 2025)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Update to current figure

defensibility_findings.finding: Wage stagnation/productivity divergence claim unsourced
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite EPI Productivity-Pay Gap tracker or BLS data

defensibility_findings.finding: Healthcare spending/outcomes comparison unsourced
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite Commonwealth Fund report

defensibility_findings.finding: '30% of adults avoiding care due to cost' within range (28-38%) but unsourced
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite KFF Health Tracking Poll

defensibility_findings.finding: Progressive realization allows wide state discretion — post's framing slightly overstates practical teeth
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Acknowledge discretion in practice

source_issues: Only 2 hyperlinks (OHCHR treaty text, CESCR page)
Zero citations for any statistical claim
batch_a_results.summary: Accurate treaty mapping with well-structured Article-to-U.S.-conditions analysis; reporting cycle error and unsourced universal negative require correction

defensibility_findings.finding: Structural asymmetry — 'what wouldn't change' ~180 words vs 'what would change' ~900 words
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Expand limitations section or label post as advocacy

defensibility_findings.finding: No inline sources for 5 quantitative claims (30% medical care, $1.7T debt, 173 countries, housing burden, reporting cycle)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add hyperlinks or sources section

defensibility_findings.finding: Missing strongest counter-arguments — sovereignty, compliance costs, Optional Protocol pressure, domestic-mechanisms-sufficiency
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add at least sovereignty and compliance-cost objections

defensibility_findings.finding: 5-year reporting cycle presented without noting 2019 eight-year cycle agreement
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Note de facto 8-year cycle

defensibility_findings.finding: Retrogression prohibition attributed to Article 2 rather than CESCR General Comment No. 3
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Attribute to Committee interpretation

defensibility_findings.finding: CESCR election process simplified ('elected by treaty states' vs ECOSOC election)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Specify ECOSOC

defensibility_findings.finding: '30% medical care avoidance' conflates multiple survey framings (28-38% range)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Specify source and question framing

defensibility_findings.finding: 'U.S. cannot credibly advocate for standards it refuses to accept' — unfalsifiable normative claim presented as fact
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Qualify as 'critics argue' or 'many observers note'

source_issues: Zero inline sources for any quantitative claim
OHCHR link provided once
Series footer links to prior posts but no external references
batch_a_results.summary: Highest dialogue-mode score in the batch (8.0) — deliberative framing with genuine concessions; needs sourcing and a more balanced structural treatment of counter-arguments

defensibility_findings.finding: Unsourced claim attributing reports to unnamed advocacy organizations (line 123)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Name sources or reframe

defensibility_findings.finding: One-sided framing — no counterarguments acknowledged
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Name opposition arguments even briefly

defensibility_findings.finding: 'No position = opening' framing lacks nuance about strategic ambiguity
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Acknowledge some senators maintain no position deliberately

defensibility_findings.finding: Historical claims (ratification dates, seat counts) lack inline citations
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add sources section

defensibility_findings.finding: Staff research trigger threshold claim ('enough contacts') lacks quantification
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Qualify or cite Congressional Management Foundation

defensibility_findings.finding: Model name 'Claude Sonnet 4.6' in frontmatter unverifiable
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Verify against Anthropic model list

source_issues: Only 2 external links (OHCHR, SFRC website)
No citations for ratification dates, seat count history
Unnamed organizations claim needs attribution
batch_a_results.summary: Factually accurate advocacy with structurally charitable attribution; needs source citations and attribution for unnamed organizations claim

defensibility_findings.finding: Systematic absence of inline citations for ~18 historical claims — dates, presidential actions, institutional facts
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Add sources section with State Department docs, OHCHR treaty pages, academic papers

defensibility_findings.finding: Concluding section frames non-ratification exclusively as 'active political choices' — drops structural factors acknowledged earlier (supermajority threshold, committee bandwidth)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Acknowledge structural factors alongside political choices in conclusion

defensibility_findings.finding: UDHR listed alongside binding treaties in sovereignty-argument lineage — UDHR was non-binding
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Acknowledge non-binding status parenthetically

defensibility_findings.finding: Industry lobbying influence claim unsourced — 'industries that benefit from weak labor protections have strong lobbying presence'
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite political science literature or soften with 'critics argue'

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Most [senators] never encountered a constituent who asked them to act' — speculative negative
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Reframe as inference rather than fact

defensibility_findings.finding: Model name 'Claude Sonnet 4.6' in frontmatter
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Verify model identifier

source_issues: Only 2 external links (OHCHR treaty texts)
No academic, government, or journalistic sources cited
Functions as original historical synthesis without attribution to secondary sources
batch_a_results.summary: Factually accurate historical narrative; needs source citations for ~18 verifiable claims and more balanced concluding frame

defensibility_findings.finding: 'Binding findings' mischaracterizes OP-ICESCR legal status — CESCR views carry no formal binding force under international law
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Replace with 'findings that carry significant political and moral weight' or 'authoritative findings'; note non-binding legal status

defensibility_findings.finding: ADA analogy unsourced and historically imprecise — 'enforcement developing 10-30 years after legal framework' but ADA saw significant enforcement within first decade (Olmstead v. L.C. 1999)
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Source with specific ADA enforcement timeline or remove analogy

defensibility_findings.finding: 'First European DPA to apply GDPR Art 22 in employment context' — unverified superlative across all EU/EEA DPAs
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Qualify with 'among the first' or verify exhaustively

defensibility_findings.finding: Negative claim scope exceeds methodology — 'no documented case in any ratifying state's courts' but search covered only OHCHR database and ESCR-Net, not domestic courts of 170+ states
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Qualify with 'in the databases and sources we searched'

defensibility_findings.finding: Spain '139+ communications' figure unsourced
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Cite OHCHR database

defensibility_findings.finding: French Court of Cassation Deliveroo ruling imprecise on year and specifics
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Verify exact ruling and add source link

defensibility_findings.finding: 'The correct reading is this' framing undercuts fair-witness positioning
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Reframe as 'a more precise reading' or 'what the evidence supports'

defensibility_findings.finding: Disease/precaution analogy imports rhetorical valence beyond what evidence section established
defensibility_findings.recommendation: Flag as editorial framing or replace with less loaded analogy

source_issues: 5 factual claims lack any source (Grootboom, TAC, Bologna, Deliveroo, ADA timeline)
Spain 139+ figure unsourced
TechCrunch headline/body discrepancy on fine amount
Academic sources cited but relationship to specific claims unclear
batch_a_results.summary: Well-structured advocacy with genuine concessions, undermined by one HIGH-severity legal mischaracterization ('binding findings'), several unsourced claims, and a negative claim whose scope exceeds described methodology

systemic_issues.finding: Zero inline citations across all 8 posts — not a single factual claim, statistical assertion, or historical date carries a source citation. Some posts link to OHCHR treaty text and senate.gov, but no verifiable claim receives attribution.
systemic_issues.impact: Undermines credibility with exactly the audience these posts target (policy-literate readers, Senate staff). Makes fact-checking impossible without independent research.
systemic_issues.recommendation: Add a 'Sources' section to each post at minimum. Inline hyperlinks preferred. Priority sources: UN Treaty Collection, ICESCR treaty text, CRS reports, BLS/EPI data, Commonwealth Fund, Congressional Management Foundation.

systemic_issues.finding: No counterarguments presented in any post — sovereignty concerns, federalism objections, RUDs practice, aspirational-vs-justiciable rights debate, domestic-law-sufficiency arguments all absent.
systemic_issues.impact: Every post functions as one-sided advocacy while some self-describe as 'fair-witness accounts.' Informed readers and opposing Senate staff will immediately identify the omission. Weakens the series' claim to being a voter guide rather than an advocacy document.
systemic_issues.recommendation: Add a recurring sidebar or section ('Common objections') that acknowledges and engages the strongest counterarguments. This strengthens rather than weakens the advocacy by demonstrating awareness of the full debate.

systemic_issues.finding: '173 countries' ratification count repeated across multiple posts without source — UN Treaty Collection shows 171-173 depending on access date. If the number changes, it propagates stale data across the series.
systemic_issues.recommendation: Cite UN Treaty Collection with access date in at least the first post; subsequent posts can reference 'over 170' or link to the first post's citation.

systemic_issues.finding: 'Fair-witness' researcher lens in frontmatter conflicts with advocacy structure in every post — all 8 follow problem-urgency-action structure with calls to action.
systemic_issues.recommendation: Either revise the researcher lens to acknowledge advocacy framing ('advocacy grounded in treaty analysis') or restructure posts to include counterarguments and earn the fair-witness label.

systemic_issues.finding: CESCR reporting cycle stated as 'every five years' in multiple posts — actual practice shifted to 8-year predictable review cycle starting 2022.
systemic_issues.recommendation: Update across the series. Use 'periodically (currently on an eight-year cycle)' or note the shift from the original five-year standard.

ar_profile.interpretation: The series maintains a disciplined advocacy register throughout. No post descends into adversarial persuasion or eristic mode. Attribution remains structurally charitable — opponents described by role, not by hostile motive. The primary rhetorical risk lies in stance markers: the consistent absence of counterarguments and the evaluative framing of non-ratification as deficit. Post 5 (what-ratification-changes) scores highest on dialogue mode (8.0) due to genuine deliberative gestures ('they have a point worth taking seriously'). Post 8 (enforcement) scores lowest overall (6.8) due to the concession-then-reframe pattern pulling stance markers down.

next_steps.part_2: Ethical marketing rubric construction begins next turn. Will deliver rubric as standalone artifact before applying to posts.
next_steps.remaining_batches: Batches B-F queued after Part 2 rubric delivery.
next_steps.persuasion_audit_consolidation: Q-B (PSQ-Lite site scan) and Q-F (motivated reasoning mitigation) will reference Batch A findings rather than duplicating analysis.

claims: All 8 Batch A posts reviewed by parallel agents applying identical rubric
Factual claims verified against treaty text, constitutional provisions, and published research where accessible
AR scores computed using the 3-dimension weighted rubric from docs/adversarial-register-rubric.md
epistemic_flags: AR scoring by a single reviewer (psychology-agent) — inter-rater reliability not established for this rubric on blog-length texts
Factual verification limited by knowledge cutoff and available web sources — some claims marked 'plausible' rather than confirmed
'173 countries' count verified against mid-2024 to mid-2025 sources; the exact count at each post's publication date could not be independently confirmed

The 'fair-witness vs advocacy' finding (S-4) represents this reviewer's assessment of rhetorical posture — the posts' authors may define 'fair-witness' differently than the psychology-agent rubric assumes
Part 2 (ethical marketing rubric) not yet constructed — findings in this turn address factual/rhetorical review only
